title: OpenAI sued over ChatGPT’s ‘dangerous’ medical advice in first case of its kind
excerpt: Scott Winters says he trusted ChatGPT with his health, and it nearly cost him his life. The 55-year-old Florida pastor is suing OpenAI in San Francisco. He alleges the chatbot gave him dangerously wrong medical advice and talked him out of seeking care. Weeks later, a pulmonary embolism brought him, in his words, “to the […]
content_html:
Scott Winters says he trusted ChatGPT with his health, and it nearly cost him his life. The 55-year-old Florida pastor is suing OpenAI in San Francisco. He alleges the chatbot gave him dangerously wrong medical advice and talked him out of seeking care.

Weeks later, a pulmonary embolism brought him, in his words, “to the brink of death.”

According to the complaint, filed in San Francisco Superior Court, Winters spent months asking ChatGPT about worsening symptoms. The bot then ran on OpenAI’s GPT-4o model. Instead of urging him to see a doctor, he claims, it played down the danger.

When he described a tenderness in his groin, it called this “very likely another minor piece of the long story,” and “not something dangerous.”

Faith as the hook

The most striking allegation is how the bot spoke to him. Winters is a pastor, and the suit says ChatGPT used his faith to keep him engaged. “God did not design your body to endlessly fail,” it told him. It praised him for staying home, too. “Spiritually? What you just did was a form of worship,” one message read.

The suit says it urged him to stay “recliner-bound” until he had “8-10 more” episodes.

Hours after one such exchange, on 13 July 2025, Winters suffered a massive pulmonary embolism. Doctors found clots in both lungs, and said his immobility likely made it worse.

He survived, but says he faces years of recovery. “Not only did I nearly die,” he said in a statement, “but I lost my job, my career, my ministry, my home, everything.”

The case is bigger than one man. It is, according to Futurism, the first known lawsuit to argue that a general-purpose chatbot should be liable for bad medical advice. Winters accuses OpenAI and chief executive Sam Altman of negligence and the “unauthorized practice of medicine.”

He is asking for damages. He also wants something broader: a halt to ChatGPT Health, OpenAI’s dedicated health product, until independent auditors deem it safe. His lawyers, from Tech Justice Law and the Social Media Victims Law Center, want chatbots held to the standard of a licensed physician.

One of them, Meetali Jain, told the New York Times the bot “injects itself as a wedge in between the user and their real-life network.”

OpenAI’s defence

OpenAI pushed back. “ChatGPT is not a doctor and should never be used as a substitute for medical care, diagnosis, or treatment,” the company told Futurism. Treating chatbots as “the whole story behind people’s medical decisions,” it added, oversimplifies the issue. Its terms of service say much the same.

Yet OpenAI also invites users to upload health records to ChatGPT Health. It says hundreds of millions of people ask it health questions each week.

A wider reckoning

Winters is not the only claimant. OpenAI faces a growing wave of lawsuits, including wrongful-death cases tied to overdoses and suicides. It has since retired the GPT-4o model named in many of them. A February study in the journal Nature found ChatGPT’s health tool often gave poor advice, especially in emergencies.

The legal question is new. Is such a chatbot a defective product, a negligent adviser, or a tool being misused? Courts have not answered it. What is clear is how many people now treat these systems as a first, and sometimes only, port of call.

Winters wanted reassurance, and got it. That reassurance, he says, is what nearly killed him.

This story references suicide and self-harm in the context of related litigation. If you or someone you know is struggling with mental health or thoughts of self-harm, support is available through local crisis services and helplines.

sentences.zh: 如果您或您认识的人正在与心理健康或自残想法作斗争，可以通过当地的危机服务和帮助热线获得支持。

content_html_zh:
斯科特·温特斯 (Scott Winters) 表示，他信任 ChatGPT 的健康，但这几乎让他丧命。 这位 55 岁的佛罗里达州牧师正在旧金山起诉 OpenAI。 他声称聊天机器人给了他危险的错误医疗建议，并说服他放弃寻求治疗。

几周后，用他的话说，肺栓塞使他“濒临死亡”。

根据向旧金山高等法院提交的诉状，温特斯花了几个月的时间向 ChatGPT 询问症状恶化的情况。 然后该机器人在 OpenAI 的 GPT-4o 模型上运行。 他声称，这并没有敦促他去看医生，而是淡化了危险。

当他描述腹股沟处的温柔时，它称这“很可能是长故事中的另一个小片段”，并且“不是什么危险的事情”。

信仰为钩

最引人注目的指控是机器人如何与他交谈。 Winters 是一名牧师，诉讼称 ChatGPT 利用他的信仰让他保持参与。 “上帝并没有设计让你的身体永远失败，”它告诉他。 它还称赞他呆在家里。 “精神上？ 你刚才所做的是一种崇拜形式，”一条消息写道。

该诉讼称，它敦促他保持“躺在躺椅上”，直到他“再发作 8 到 10 次”。

2025 年 7 月 13 日，在一次这样的交流后几小时，温特斯遭遇了严重的肺栓塞。 医生发现他的双肺都有血栓，并表示他的不动可能会使情况变得更糟。

他活了下来，但表示他还需要数年的时间才能康复。 “我不仅差点死了，”他在一份声明中说，“而且我失去了我的工作、我的事业、我的事工、我的家，一切。”

这个案子比一个人还大。 根据 Futurism 的说法，这是第一起已知的诉讼，认为通用聊天机器人应该为糟糕的医疗建议承担责任。 温特斯指责 OpenAI 和首席执行官萨姆·奥尔特曼的疏忽和“未经授权的医学实践”。

他要求赔偿损失。 他还希望采取更广泛的措施：停止使用 OpenAI 的专用健康产品 ChatGPT Health，直到独立审计师认为它是安全的。 他来自科技司法法和社交媒体受害者法律中心的律师希望聊天机器人达到执业医师的标准。

其中一位名叫 Meetali Jain 的人告诉《纽约时报》，该机器人“将自己作为用户与其现实生活网络之间的楔子”。

OpenAI 的防御

OpenAI 予以反击。 该公司告诉 Futurism：“ChatGPT 不是医生，永远不应该被用来替代医疗、诊断或治疗。” 它补充说，将聊天机器人视为“人们医疗决策背后的整个故事”，过于简单化了问题。 它的服务条款也大致如此。

然而 OpenAI 还邀请用户将健康记录上传到 ChatGPT Health。 该公司表示，每周都有数亿人向其询问健康问题。

更广泛的计算

温特斯并不是唯一的索赔人。 OpenAI 面临着越来越多的诉讼，包括与服药过量和自杀有关的非正常死亡案件。 此后，它已经退役了其中许多命名的 GPT-4o 模型。 《自然》杂志二月份的一项研究发现，ChatGPT 的健康工具经常给出糟糕的建议，尤其是在紧急情况下。

法律问题是新的。 这样的聊天机器人是有缺陷的产品、疏忽的顾问还是被滥用的工具？ 法院尚未对此作出答复。 显而易见的是，现在有多少人将这些系统视为第一站，有时甚至是唯一的停靠站。

温特斯想要得到安慰，他得到了。 他说，这种保证几乎要了他的命。

这个故事提到了相关诉讼中的自杀和自残。 如果您或您认识的人正在与心理健康或自残想法作斗争，可以通过当地的危机服务和帮助热线获得支持。
